coredata.dc:title: Hydrophilic P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel for visual detection and removal metal ions in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongdong
Li, Huanhuan
Li, Jingjing
Xu, Ziang
Liu, Haozhe
Zhao, Yiping
Feng, Xia
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal are harmful to the mankind and difficult to observe in water, so it is necessary for the detection and removal of metal ions. Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent materials for detecting metal ions and their modified nanocomposites have great potential for water treatment applications. Our research is developing a dual material of detecting and adsorbing metal ions based on poly acrylamide-co-CD-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (poly (Am-CD-AMPS)) by inverse miniemulsion polymerization. Firstly, CDs were functionalized with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to obtain double bond which can be stably immobilized into the microgels network during the polymerization. The prepared poly (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels demonstrated the blue fluorescent property and excitation-dependent behavior under UV light and Fluorescence spectrophotometer. The microgels had sensitivity toward Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ and detection limit as low as 2.3 nM, 3.2 μM and 1.3 μM. Meanwhile, poly (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels had adsorption capacity of 102.13 mg·g−1 and 85.00 mg·g−1 toward to Fe3+ and Cu2+, and detailing of the possible mechanistic insight into the ion adsorption was also discussed. Reusability measurements showed that the synthesized microgels had the better stability in application of detecting and adsorbing metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microgel
Carbon dots
Detection
Ion adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel for Cu2+ and Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number. 21374078 ), the Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (grant numbers15JCYBJC17900, 15PTSYJC00250 and 17YFZCSF01230 ), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (grant number. IRT-17R80 ) and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (grant number. TD13-5044 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
(a) UV/Vis absorption of CDs solution, (b) TEM images and (c) size distribution histogram.
(a) XPS wide-scan of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel; (b) C1s core-level XPS spectra; (c) O1s core-level XPS spectrum; (d) S2p core-level XPS spectrum; (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs, PCD and P(Am-CD-AMPS).
(a) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and (b) TEM images of p(Am-CD-AMPS).
Fluorescence spectra of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel monitored (a) at different excitation wavelengths, (b) at different concentrations.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel at different metal ions solution, and (b) Comparison of photographs of P(Am-CD-AMPS)microgel before and after addition of Cu2+ under Day light and UV light.

Fluorescence spectra of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel with different concentrations of (a) Hg2+, (d) Fe3+, (g) Cu2+; The linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio F0/F-1 and the concentration of (b) and (c) Hg2+, (e) and (f) Fe3+, (h) and (i) Cu2+.
Effect of solution pH on adsorption of Cu2+ and Fe3+ by P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
Absorption kinetic curve of Cu2+ and Fe3+ on P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
(a) Pseudo-first-order and (b) Pseudo-second-order kinetics plots for Cu2+ and Fe3+ on P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
The effect of initial feed heavy metal concentration ions on the adsorption capacity of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
The models of adsorption isotherm: (a) Langumir, (b) Freundlich.
(a) Regenrated adsorption capacity, (b) Regenrated fluorescence capacity and (c) quenching mechanism of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel.
Comparison of the adsorption of Cu2+ ions with different materials.

head.ce:title: Hydrophilic P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel for visual detection and removal metal ions in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: D. Zhang and H. Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal are harmful to the mankind and difficult to observe in water, so it is necessary for the detection and removal of metal ions. Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent materials for detecting metal ions and their modified nanocomposites have great potential for water treatment applications. Our research is developing a dual material of detecting and adsorbing metal ions based on poly acrylamide-co-CD-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (poly (Am-CD-AMPS)) by inverse miniemulsion polymerization. Firstly, CDs were functionalized with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to obtain double bond which can be stably immobilized into the microgels network during the polymerization. The prepared poly (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels demonstrated the blue fluorescent property and excitation-dependent behavior under UV light and Fluorescence spectrophotometer. The microgels had sensitivity toward Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ and detection limit as low as 2.3 nM, 3.2 μM and 1.3 μM. Meanwhile, poly (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels had adsorption capacity of 102.13 mg·g−1 and 85.00 mg·g−1 toward to Fe3+ and Cu2+, and detailing of the possible mechanistic insight into the ion adsorption was also discussed. Reusability measurements showed that the synthesized microgels had the better stability in application of detecting and adsorbing metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination by toxic heavy metals such as Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ is becoming an increasingly important issue in separation science and environmental remediation, as these metals are harmful to humans and other species [1]. Increasing amounts of mercury are discarded into the environment (air, soil and water) due to their universally use in the pharmaceutical and paper industries, electric battery production, amalgam dental fillings and combustion of coal [2]. Effective reduction of heavy metals down to trace levels (e.g., less than 5 ppb) remains a great challenge. For those existing issues, new highly efficient and cheaper adsorbents or sensors attract the attention of researchers [3].

At present, how to identify heavy metal ions in sewage has become the most important issue in the treatment of environmental water pollution. Researchers have developed many methods for sensing metal ions, including optical methods, capillary electrophoresis, electrochemical methods, atomic absorption spectrometry, and so on [4]. However, most of these methods show some limitations, such as complicated processing, high cost instruments and time consuming operations [5–8]. Therefore, simple and low cost methods for the detection of metal ions are imperative. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have captivated the attention of scientists due to their unique properties, such as robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, good water solubility, etc [9–12]. CDs have recently been widely applied in various fields, including bio-imaging, sensors, photo catalysis, and so on. The fluorescent detection of metal ions is one of the most important applications [13]. The good photoexcitation fluorescence stability of the CDs ensures the accuracy of the test results. And there is no need to worry about their poisoning effect on human health and the environment [14]. Hence, CDs exhibit great promise in the fluorescent detection of metal ions and many case studies have been explored and investigated [15,16].

In addition, the CDs are extremely hydrophilic, which is not suitable for industrial applications. When fluorescent CDs were used to detect metal ions in water solution which were difficult to remove from water due to its super hydrophilicity. It is necessary to find a carrier to fix the carbon dots and avoid secondary pollution. For several decades, hydrophilic hydrogels had received considerable attention, as specific sorbents, in many applications [17]. Such as poly (acrylamide) based materials are used in enhanced oil recovery. Generally, polymers that can resist the harsh conditions such as high temperature, high salt concentration and presence of bivalent cations are scarce [18,19]. As heavy metal ion adsorbent, 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (AMPS), a water-soluble multifunctional anionic monomer, contains both sulfonic acid groups (-SO3H) and amide groups (-O = C-NH-) [20]. Moreover, the adsorption mechanism of -O = C-NH- and -SO3H groups on metal ions is chelation and ion exchange, respectively.

In this work, fluorescent CDs were firstly modified with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) named as PCD containing a double bond, and then a three-dimensional structure of poly(acrylamide-co-CD-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid) microgel (p(Am-CD-AMPS)) was prepared by inverse emulsion polymerization with acrylamide (AM) and AMPS. The microstructure and surface groups of the p (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels were characterized. The p(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel with dual functions of detecting and removing metal ions was investigated. The design of fluorescent CDs-based nanosensors is of great significance to the environment and human health.

Citric acid monohydrate, 1, 2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) were purchased from Tianjin Kermel. Acrylamide (Am) was obtained from Tianjin Pengyuan. 2-methylpropanesulfonic acid was supplied by Tianjin Heowns. 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (AMPS) was purchased from Shanghai Darui Fine chemical. N, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) and span 80 were acquired from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical.

Cyclohexane was purchased from Tianjin Kermel. Iso-Propyl alcohol was purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chenmical Reagent Technologies. Iron solution (1000 ppm) was purchased from National Steel Materials Testing Center. All metal ion stock solutions were prepared from sulfate salts or chlorate salts. Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) was supplied by Aladdin. All other reagents were of analytical grades and used without further purification. Ultrapure water used for the solution throughout all the experiments.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared as previous report [21] and specific process of preparation illustrated in the supplementary materials. In order to firmly fix the carbon dot in the microgel, the amino group of CDs was reacted with the epoxy group of GMA to obtain a functionalized CDS with a double bond, which was named PCD. 2 g CDs were dissolved into 20 mL ultrapure water and ultrasonic 10 min. Then 2.4 mL GMA was added to the CDs solution, stirred for 24 h at 35℃. Subsequently, the PCD was further obtained by the multipleextraction with n-hexane. Finally, the PCD was obtained by lyophilization at −60 ℃ and saved for further reactions. PCD was confirmed by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrum that showed in figure (Figs. S1 and S2).

The P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel was prepared two-phase system consisting of water and cyclohexane. In brief, a solution A was obtained by dissolved 0.80 g Am, 0.07 g PCD (obtained in the previous step), 0.07 g AMPS, 0.04 g MBAA and 0.094 g NaCl in the water with ultrasonication for 10 min. The continuous phase B was prepared by dissolved 1.2 g Span80 in 50 g cyclohexane. Then, the disperse phase A was slowly added into the continuous phase B under stirring. After being stirred for 1.5 h at room temperature, an inverse miniemulsion was obtained, which was sonicated for another 2 min. Then, 0.128 g of AIBN oil-soluble initiator was added to the emulsion to initiate the system at 65° C, and the mixture was further stirred for 2 h.

After the polymerization, the emulsion was cooled to room temperature. With the addition of isopropanol, the system became a mixed precipitate. The supernatant was then removed and the precipitate was collected. To remove the osmotic agent NaCl and other impurities which contained unreacted monomer and cross-linker, oligomers and non-crosslinked polymers, the precipitate was resuspended with ultrapure water and the dispersion was dialyzed of ultrapure water for several days. After dialysis, the product was freeze-dried to obtain P (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels. The detailed schematic was shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphologies and structures of the samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI H-7650). The size distribution of microgel was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a ZEN3490 MALVERN intruments. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co.) spectra was used to collect the surface data of the samples. The compositions of microgel were analyzed by X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS, Genesis 60S). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of the microgel was analyzed using a Hitachi S-4800 (Japan). The thermos gravimetric analysis (TG) and differential thermal gravity (DTG) were measured with STA409PC thermal analyzer under N2 atmosphere from 20 to 700 ℃ at a rate of 10 ℃/min.

The UV–Vis absorption of the microgel was measured with a Purkinje General TU-1901 UV–visible spectrometer. The fluorescence photographs of the CDs and microgel were obtained by ZF-20D dark-box-type ultraviolet analyzer under 365 nm. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs solution and microgel were recorded on F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Fluorescence assay of metal ions: the sensitivity and selectivity measurements of different metal ions by the CDs and microgel was performed at room temperature, and metal ions at a concentration of 5 × 10-3 mol/L. 2 mL of different metal ions solution was added into 1 mL CDs solution or 1 mL microgel solution, respectively. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were collected after 30 min at 360 nm or 385 nm excitation wavelength.

The adsorption behavior of microgel was all executed in conical flask with 0.01 g dry microgel submerged in 20 mL metal ion solutions. The conical flask was placed in a shaker at 25℃. The concentration of metal ions after adsorption was analyzed by atomic adsorption spectrophotometer. All experiments were conducted at least three times, and the average value of the adsorption capacity was adopted as the final results.

The pH was adjusted using 0.1 mol/L HCl and NaOH. The adsorption capacity qe (mg/g) on microgel was calculated by equation (1):
(1)
q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
)
V / m
where C0 and Ce (mg/L) represented the initial and equilibrium concentrations, respectively; V (L) was the volume of solution; m (g) was the microgel weight.

In isothermal studies, the adsorption capacity of P (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels for Fe3+ and Cu2+ between 0 min and 300 min was tested, and pseudo-primary and pseudo-secondary mechanisms were studied to determine microgel Adsorption behavior. In addition, isothermal adsorption of P (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels with metal ions with different initial concentrations (50–600 mg⋅L−1) was evaluated, and the resulted data were fitted using Langmuir and Freundlich models Data.

To accurately analyze the experimental results, a distribution function such as Chi-Square (χ2), normalized standard deviation (NSD (%)), and mean relative error (ARE) is used to evaluate the model. The Chisquare (χ2)[22], normalized standard deviation (NSD)[23] and average relative error (ARE)[24]test were applied to validate the data between the experimental and hypothesis modeling data, and the equation is described below:
(2)
χ
2
= ∑
q
e
exp
-
q
e
cal
2
q
e
cal
(3)
N S D
%
= 100 ×
∑
q
e
exp
-
q
e
cal
q
e
exp
2
N - 1
(4)
ARE =
∑
1
n
q
e . e x p
-
q
e , c a l
/
q
e , e x p
/ N
× 100
where qe exp and qe cal were experimental and hypothesis models capacity (mg/g) at equilibrium, N was the number of measures in the experimental data.

To evaluate the regeneration ability of the microgel, the process of desorption and fluorescent regeneration of microgel for heavy metal was studied for consecutive five cycles. In every cycle, the metal ion adsorbed microgel was regenerated by being added in 20 mL 0.1 M HCl solution [25] and shaken for 8 h. After each desorption, the fluorescent intensity and adsorption capacity were measured.

The UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared CDs given in Fig. 2 a showed a peak at around 320 nm and another absorption peak at 240 nm attributable to the π → π* transition [26]. A bright blue color PL emission was observed upon excitation of the aqueous solution of CDs at 360 nm ultraviolet light irradiation and illustrated in the inset in Fig. 2(a). The morphology and size of the CDs were shown in Fig. 2b and c. It was apparent from the figure that these CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical particles and the average size is 8 nm. Using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, we calculated that the quantum yield of the prepared CDs under the excitation of 360 nm was 30.2% (Fig. S3), which is much higher than that of the other carbon dots previously reported. To further explore the optical properties of the as-prepared CDs, we carried out a detailed PL study with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 to 380 nm (Fig. S4). As most other luminescent CDs, the as-prepared CDs exhibit a weak excitation-dependent PL behavior, and the PL peak remained almost unchanged (at 450 nm) for the excitation wavelength over 320–380 nm. As different energy levels associated with different ‘‘surface states’’ formed by different functional groups are responsible for the excitation-dependent-emission phenomenon [27], the observed excitation-independent emission over 320–400 nm indicates a relatively uniform and well-passivated CDs surface.

XPS was used to determine microgel surface elements composition. The XPS spectra of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel were shown in Fig. 3 (a). It can be seen from the total spectrum that the P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel mainly contains four elements of C, N, O and S. From O1s (Fig. 3(c)), the peaks at the binding energy of 531.28 eV and 532.98 eV correspond to the − OH group and the –O = C-OH/O = C group [28,29], respectively, which mainly from PCD. In Fig. 3(d), the binding energy was corresponding to S peak at 168.18 eV, mainly from AMPS. In summary, P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels were successfully prepared.

To further confirm the formation of microgel, a comparison of the FT-IR spectra of CDs, PCD, and P(Am-CD-AMPS) was further evaluated the stable existence of the CDs grafted in the microgel networks. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the prepared CDs show an apparent broad absorption peak of hydroxyl (−OH) groups at 3700–3200 cm−1 , amide (C-N) groups at 1447 cm−1 and a characteristic absorption peak at 1645 cm−1 of carbonyl groups (C = O). These hydrophilic − COOH, −NH2 and − OH groups on the CDs enable the CDs to be dispersed very well in water[30]. Compared with the CDs, new absorption peaks appear at 1710 cm−1 and 1141 cm−1 in the PCD spectrum, respectively, owing to the C = O-and C-O-C stretching vibration peaks conjugated with C = C. It was also proved that GMA was successfully grafted onto the surface of CDs, that is, PCD containing double bonds was successfully prepared. It can be seen from the PCD and P(Am-CD-AMPS) spectra that the absorption peak at 3307–3561 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of –NH-, and the stretching vibration absorption peak of C = O(amide I) at 1613 cm−1 is 1455 cm−1. It was the stretching vibration absorption peak of CN (amide III), which is the absorption peak shared by PCD and P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels[31]. The peak of the -SO3H peak at 1037 cm−1 in the c-spectrum is attributed to AMPS. In summary, it can be demonstrated that P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels have been successfully prepared.

The size distribution of microgel particles were investigated by DLS and TEM showed in Fig. 4 . The particle size distribution of p(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels was very narrow and the sizes are around 400 nm, indicating that the prepared microgels were with relatively uniform size. In order to further observe the particle size of the gel, it was tested by transmission electron microscopy. It can be seen from the TEM image that the microgel particles were spherical and the particle size is uniformly dispersed, which was consistent with the results of the SEM image (Fig S5).There was no obvious agglomeration and the size was about 360 nm, which is slightly smaller than the DLS test results. This phenomenon may be caused by different test conditions. DLS is performed in an aqueous solution, while the TEM test keeps the microspheres dry during the test, while the microgels swell slightly in water.

In order to further study the stability of the microgel, Fig. 5 showed the TGA curves of pure P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel. The result showed microgel was stable under 75.2℃, and the weight loss associated with the release of internal combined water. A significant weight loss was occurred at about 269° C, which is due to the decomposition of the sulfonic acid groups and hydroxyl groups in the microgel. The main weight loss at 382℃, due to the molecular chain in the microgel breaks and the polymer depolymerizes, finally residue with a weight percentage of less than 14%. The results show that the gel had good stability performance in the normal use temperature range.

To further explore the optical properties of the as-prepared microgel particles, we carried out a detailed PL study with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 to 410 nm (Fig. 6 a). The emission peak had a red-shifted from 429 to 450 nm, indicating that P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel had a strong dependence on the excitation wavelengths. Although, up to date, the origins of PL in CDs are not very clear, this wavelength-dependent phenomena (or multicolor PL) may come from the different distribution of emissive energy traps of CDs on the surface of P(Am-CD-AMPS). In order to obtain the best fluorescence performance, the fluorescence intensity was tested at different concentrations. Fig. 6b showed that the PL intensity firstly raised, then declined and reached maximum near the 1 mg/mL with increasing concentration of microgel. When the concentration of the fluorescence substance was increased to 2 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity tend to decline due to self-quenching [32].

Fig. 7 (a) showed that the fluorescence intensity of microgel response to representative metal ions, including Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+ with the same concentration of 100 mg/L. It was seen that fluorescence intensity of P(Am-CD-AMPS) had a much significant decrease upon addition of Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+. In contrast, no obvious change was observed by addition of other ions into the microgel solutions. In order to further observe the change of fluorescence ability more directly, the photos of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel powder and aqueous solution before and after adsorption of Cu2+ were compared under natural light (left) and ultraviolet light (right) showed in Fig. 7(b). It can be seen from the photos that the solid and aqueous solutions of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel particles were pale yellow under visible light, and emitted strong blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. After adding Cu2+, microgels and its aqueous solution can obviously observe blue fluorescence quenching under ultraviolet light, indicating that P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel can effectively response to Cu2+.

On the basis of the above discussion, the sensitivities of the P(Am-CD-AMPS) sensor for the detection of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ had been investigated. With the addition of various concentrations of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased showed in Fig. 8 (a), (d) and (g). The quenching efficiency ((F0/F-1) showed a good linear relationship versus the Hg2+ concentration ranged from 0 to 20 μM (Fig. 8(b) and (c)), where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The linear regression equations was F0/F-1 = 0.0119C and F0/F-1 = 0.00032C with a correlation coefficient of 0.9788 and 0.9660. Fig. 8(d) exhibited the fluorescence spectra of microgel solution with the addition of Fe3+. And Fig. 8(e) and (f) showed that the quenching efficiency had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ ranged from 0 to 5 mM. The linear regression equation for Fe3+ detection was F0/F-1 = 0.0211C and F0/F-1 = 0.0013C with a correlation coefficient of 0.9634 and 0.9574, where F0 and F were fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. When Cu2+ with concentration ranged from 0.5 to10000 μM was added to the microgel solution, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased (Fig. 8(g)). Fig. 8(h) and (i) presented the good linear relationship of the quenching efficiency with the concentrations of Cu2+, and the linear regression equationis F0/F-1 = 0.0539C and F0/F-1 = 0.0063C with a correlation coefficient of 0.9670 and 0.9860, where F0 and F were fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The detection limit of 2.3 nM, 3.2 μM and 1.3 μM was obtained based on 3σ/slope (where s is the standard deviation) for Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel for Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different initial pH (from 3 to 7) value on the adsorption capacity of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel were shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen from the figure that as the pH value increases, the adsorption of metal ions on the microgel first increases, then decreases, and its adsorption capacity for Fe3+ and Cu2+ reaches the highest value at pH = 5, respectively (102.13 mg · g−1 and 85.00 mg · g−1). This is because the hydrogen hydrate (H3O+) has a competitive relationship with metal ions at lower pH values, and its binding capacity with the adsorbent adsorption site is greater than that of metal ions, which inhibits the binding of metal ions, resulting in a lower adsorption of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by P (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels[33,34]. When pH = 5, together with OH, COOH with a pKa of about 5 adsorbs Cu2+ and Fe3+ through chelation, resulting in an increase in the amount of metal ions adsorbed by the microgel [35]. Under weakly acidic (pH close to 6) conditions, amino groups (NH2), −CONH2 have more positive charges due to the loss of protons in the aqueous solution, leading to a decrease in adsorption sites. When the pH value is 7, the spontaneous precipitation of heavy metal ions reduces the adsorption of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by P (Am-CD-AMPS) microgels [36]. The P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel morphology before and after adsorption of metal ions and desorption of heavy metal ions showed in Fig. S5.

In order to study the adsorption rate and pathway from the adsorption to equilibrium process, the adsorption kinetics of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions by P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels were also investigated (Fig. 10 ). With the increase of time, the adsorption capacity of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels on Fe3+ and Cu2+ reached adsorption equilibrium at 30 min and 50 min, respectively. The adsorption equilibrium was reached within short time, that is, the P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel had a fast adsorption rate for Fe3+ and Cu2+. During the adsorption process, when the microgel was initially added to the heavy metal ion solution, the –NH2, –COOH, –OH, and -SO3H on the surface of the microgel begin to chelate with the metal ion. Along with the swelling of the microgel, the amount of adsorption rapidly increased until the adsorption site of the microgel became saturated and the swelling reached equilibrium.

The rate of adsorption was always determined by using two different rate equations. Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order mechanisms were investigated for determining adsorption behaviors. The comparison was then drawn between the experimental and calculated data. The two kinetic rate equations (Eqs. (3) and (4))[37] can be written as follows:

Pseudo-first-order:
(3)
lg
(
q e
-
q t
)
= lg
q e
-
k 1
2.303
t

Pseudo-second-order:
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
+
1
q e
t
where qe (mg/g) is the amount per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium, and qt (mg/g) is the metal ions adsorbed at time t, while k1 (min−1) and k2 (g/mg⋅min−1) are equilibrium rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption interactions, respectively.
According to the adsorption kinetic curve, ln (qe-qt) and t/qt were plotted against t, respectively, and the fitting curve shown in Fig. 11 was obtained. The fitting results were shown in Table S1.

It can be seen from the fitting results that the equilibrium adsorption amount of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels for the two metal ions was close to that of the experimental result, and the linear correlation was suitable for the adsorption kinetics of Fe3+ and Cu2+. The coefficient R2 had a higher parameter when using a quasi-secondary dynamics model. Hence, the adsorption kinetics of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels was closer to quasi-secondary dynamics model. The quasi-first-order kinetic model is based on a simple model of Lagergren which applied to the adsorption process in the liquid phase, and the quasi-second-order kinetic model is based on the adsorption rate based on chemisorption, involving electron sharing or electron transfer between the adsorbents. In summary, P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels dominate the chemical adsorption of two metal ions by coordination or chelation of amino groups, hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and sulfonic groups.

Fig. 12 presented adsorption isotherms of Fe3+ and Cu2+ on P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels, where the adsorption capacities under initial concentrations from 50 to 600 mg⋅L−1 were measured. To explore the adsorption behavior and obtain valuable insights of surface features, Langmuir and Freundlich models (eq5 and eq6)[38] were applied to fit these adsorption data (Fig. 13 (a) and (b)).

Langmuir:
(5)
c e
q e
=
c e
q m
+
1
K L
q m

Freundlich:
(6)
ln
q e
= ln
K F
+
1 n
ln
c e
where qe (mg⋅g−1) means the adsorption capacity of Fe3+ and Cu2+ at equilibrium, qm (mg⋅g−1) means the maximum adsorption capacity, KL (L·mg−1) means the Langmuir constant related to adsorption energy, Ce (mg⋅L−1) means the equilibrium concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+ solution, and KF and n are Freundlich constants related to adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity. Table S2 concluded all corresponding parameters in Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models.

According to compare the correlation coefficients (R2), it was found that the adsorption of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels for Cu2+ were fitted Langmuir model very well (R2＞0.95) in Table S1, indicating an homogeneous characteristics on its surface. The obtained experimental values and model values were verified by Chi-square (χ2), normalized standard deviation (NSD (%)) and the average relative error (ARE), which were calculated by the formula (1), (2) (3) and collected in Table S3. The values calculated from pseudo-second-order model was smaller than that of pseudo-first-order, implying chemical adsorption of two metal ions by coordination or chelation of amino groups, hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and sulfonic groups. However, for Adsorption isotherms, although Fe3+ has a larger R2, other small values indicate that it is more consistent with the Freundlich model.

In order to evaluate the microgel fluorescence reversibility and adsorption reusability performance, the saturated microgel was desorbed with hydrochloric acid solution, and its cycle fluorescence intensity and adsorption performance were measured [39]. Five regenerated adsorption capacity and reversibility of fluorescence results of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel were showed in Fig. 14 . The amount of P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel adsorption to Cu2+ was 85.00 mg·g−1. The experimental data of cyclic adsorption of Cu2+ on micro-gels showed that the recovery rate of five adsorptions remained at 84.7% to 94.1%, indicating that microgels have good durability and reusability. The hydrochloric acid eluent has a very effective desorption effect on Cu2+, mainly because the pH value of the hydrochloric acid solution is low, the H+ content in the solution is high, and the amino group in the microgel is protonated, which is convenient for removing heavy metal ions. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured after adsorption and desorption for 5 times, and the fluorescence intensity was maintained at 80% or more showed in Fig. 14(b). From the above fluorescence quenching mechanism, it was found that after the metal ions forming a complex with the carbon dots were removed, the fluorescence was restored illustrated in Fig. 14(c). However, the quenching of fluorescence is not restored due to the non-radiative recombination of excitons caused by electron transfer, so that the fluorescence intensity after each adsorption/desorption is decreased. During the test, after the fourth cycle, the fluorescence intensity increased, which may be due to factors such as uneven dispersion of the microgel. Finally, we compared the amount of copper ion adsorption with the previous literature. The results are shown in Table 1 .

A novel fluorescent P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel for sensing and adsorbing metal was successfully developed. The spherical particle size distribution of p(Am-CD-AMPS) microgels was very narrow and the sizes were around 400 nm. The prepared microgel with fluorescent stability possessed good sensitivity for Hg2+, Fe3+ , Cu2+. At the same time, the detection limit measured to the abovementioned three type of ions was 2.3 nM, 3.2 μM and 1.3 μM, respectively. The thermal stability allowed the gel microspheres to be used within the temperature range of the general application. The adsorption amount of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 102.13 mg·g−1, 85.00 mg·g−1 at optimal pH = 5. Adsorption kinetic results indicated that P(Am-CD-AMPS) microgel conforms to quasi-secondary dynamics model, tending to chemical adsorption. After the metal ions formed as a complex with the carbon dots were removed by HCl solution, the fluorescence ability was restored. The reversibility and reusability experiments showed that the microgel had good stability for five cycles. We expect that CD-based sensors will have application value in environmental treatment.

Dongdong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Huanhuan Li: Writing - original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Jingjing Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Ziang Xu: Validation, Formal analysis. Haozhe Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Yiping Zhao: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Xia Feng: Writing - review & editing. Li Chen: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number.21374078), the Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (grant numbers15JCYBJC17900, 15PTSYJC00250 and 17YFZCSF01230), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (grant number. IRT-17R80) and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (grant number. TD13-5044).

No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145668.
